body:
UX & UI Design Bible

Gestalt principles, accessibility & design tokens

UX/UI principles every frontend developer must know. Design decisions directly affect code architecture.

Core UX Principles

1. Heuristic Evaluation (Nielsen's 10 Heuristics)

Visibility of system status: Always show loading states, progress bars, success/error feedback. Never leave the user guessing.

Match between system and real world: Use familiar language, icons, and metaphors. A trash can icon for delete, not an X.

User control and freedom: Provide undo, redo, cancel, back. Let users recover from mistakes easily.

Consistency and standards: Same action = same result everywhere. Follow platform conventions (links are blue, buttons look clickable).

Error prevention: Disable submit until form is valid, confirm destructive actions, auto-save drafts.

Recognition over recall: Show options rather than making users remember. Autocomplete, recent items, breadcrumbs.

Flexibility and efficiency: Keyboard shortcuts for power users, simplified flows for beginners.

Aesthetic and minimalist design: Every extra element competes for attention. Remove what doesn't serve a purpose.

Help users recognize and recover from errors: Clear error messages with actionable solutions, not cryptic codes.

Help and documentation: Contextual tooltips, onboarding flows, searchable help.

These 10 heuristics are used in UX audits at companies like Google, Meta, and Amazon. Walk through each heuristic against your product to find issues. Interviewers often ask 'how would you improve the UX of X?' — use these as your framework.

Interaction Design Patterns

Loading States

Skeleton screens: Grey placeholder shapes mimicking content layout. Better than spinners because they show WHERE content will appear, reducing perceived load time by up to 50%.

Optimistic updates: Show the result immediately, sync in background. Like button turns red instantly — don't wait for server.

Progressive loading: Show content as it arrives (streaming SSR). User sees header + nav immediately, then content streams in.

Stale-while-revalidate: Show cached data immediately, refresh in background. TanStack Query does this automatically.

Feedback Patterns

Micro-interactions: Button press animation, toggle slide, checkbox bounce. Tiny details that make the UI feel alive.

Toast notifications: Temporary, non-blocking feedback. Auto-dismiss after 3-5s. Stack from bottom. Include undo for destructive actions.

Inline validation: Validate on blur (not keystroke). Show error below field. Green checkmark for valid. Don't validate empty fields until submit attempt.

Progress indicators: Determinate (progress bar with %) for known durations. Indeterminate (spinner) for unknown. Step indicators for multi-step flows.

The best loading state is no loading state. Use optimistic updates + skeleton screens + prefetching to make the app feel instant. Only show spinners for operations that truly take time (file uploads, complex calculations).

Responsive & Adaptive Design

Responsive Design Principles

Mobile-first: Design for smallest screen first, then enhance for larger screens. This forces you to prioritize content and simplify.

Breakpoints: Don't design for devices — design for content. Add a breakpoint when the layout breaks, not at iPhone/iPad sizes. Common: 640px (sm), 768px (md), 1024px (lg), 1280px (xl).

Touch targets: Minimum 44×44px (Apple) or 48×48px (Google). Fingers are imprecise — small buttons cause frustration.

Content priority: On mobile, stack vertically and hide secondary content behind toggles/accordions. Navigation → hamburger menu or bottom nav.

Adaptive Patterns

Container queries: Components adapt to their container, not viewport. A card in a sidebar looks different from the same card in main content.

Fluid typography: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) — scales smoothly without breakpoints.

Responsive images: srcset + sizes for resolution, <picture> for art direction (different crops per viewport).

Accessibility (a11y) for Developers

WCAG Levels

Level A: Minimum. Text alternatives for images, keyboard navigable, no seizure-causing content.

Level AA: Standard target. Color contrast 4.5:1 (text), 3:1 (large text), resize to 200% without loss, focus visible, error identification.

Level AAA: Enhanced. Contrast 7:1, no time limits, sign language for video. Rarely required but ideal.

Developer Checklist

Semantic HTML: Use <button> not <div onClick>. Use <nav>, <main>, <header>.

Keyboard navigation: Everything usable with Tab, Enter, Space, Escape, Arrow keys. Focus visible (never outline: none without replacement).

Color: Never use color alone to convey information. Add icons, text, or patterns alongside color.

Images: alt text for meaningful images, alt="" for decorative ones.

Forms: Labels linked to inputs, error messages associated with aria-describedby, fieldset/legend for groups.

Dynamic content: aria-live regions for updates, manage focus when content changes (modals, route transitions).

The #1 accessibility mistake: using <div> with onClick instead of <button>. A div has NO keyboard support, NO role, NO focus management. A button gets all of that for free. Use the right element and you get accessibility for free.

Design systems and design tokens — the bridge between design and engineering.

Design Tokens — Complete Guide

Design tokens are the atomic values of a design system — the single source of truth for colors, spacing, typography, shadows, borders, and animation.

Token Hierarchy

/* TIER 1: Global/Primitive tokens (raw values) */
--color-blue-500: #3b82f6;
--color-blue-600: #2563eb;
--color-gray-100: #f3f4f6;
--spacing-4: 1rem;
--spacing-8: 2rem;
--font-size-sm: 0.875rem;
--radius-md: 8px;

/* TIER 2: Semantic/Alias tokens (purpose-driven) */
--color-primary: var(--color-blue-600);
--color-text: var(--color-gray-900);
--color-text-muted: var(--color-gray-500);
--color-surface: var(--color-white);
--color-border: var(--color-gray-200);
--color-error: var(--color-red-600);
--color-success: var(--color-green-600);

/* TIER 3: Component tokens (specific usage) */
--button-bg: var(--color-primary);
--button-text: var(--color-white);
--button-radius: var(--radius-md);
--button-padding: var(--spacing-2) var(--spacing-4);
--card-bg: var(--color-surface);
--card-border: var(--color-border);
--card-shadow: 0 1px 3px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.1);
Think of tokens like a language translation chain: Global tokens are the dictionary (blue-500 = #3b82f6). Semantic tokens give meaning (primary = blue-600). Component tokens are sentences (button-bg = primary). Changing 'primary' in one place updates every component that uses it.

Theming with Tokens

/* Light theme (default) */
:root {{
  --color-bg: #ffffff;
  --color-text: #1f2937;
  --color-surface: #f9fafb;
  --color-primary: #3b82f6;
}}

/* Dark theme */
[data-theme="dark"] {{
  --color-bg: #0f172a;
  --color-text: #e2e8f0;
  --color-surface: #1e293b;
  --color-primary: #60a5fa;
}}

/* Auto from system preference */
@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {{
  :root:not([data-theme]) {{
    --color-bg: #0f172a;
    /* ... dark values */
  }}
}}

/* Components just use semantic tokens — theme-agnostic! */
.card {{
  background: var(--color-surface);
  color: var(--color-text);
  border: 1px solid var(--color-border);
}}

Design System Architecture

Component API Design

// Good component API — composable, predictable
<Button
  variant="primary" | "secondary" | "ghost" | "destructive"
  size="sm" | "md" | "lg"
  disabled={{boolean}}
  loading={{boolean}}
  leftIcon={{ReactNode}}
  asChild  // render as child element (Radix pattern)
>
  Label
</Button>

// Composable pattern (Radix/shadcn style)
<Dialog>
  <Dialog.Trigger asChild>
    <Button>Open</Button>
  </Dialog.Trigger>
  <Dialog.Content>
    <Dialog.Title>Confirm</Dialog.Title>
    <Dialog.Description>Are you sure?</Dialog.Description>
    <Dialog.Close />
  </Dialog.Content>
</Dialog>

Design System Tooling

Storybook: Component playground — each component has stories showing all variants, states, and edge cases. Enables visual testing, documentation, and design review.

Chromatic: Visual regression testing — screenshots every component on every PR, highlights visual diffs.

Figma tokens plugin: Sync design tokens between Figma and code. Designers change a color → code updates automatically.

Style Dictionary: Transform tokens from JSON into CSS variables, SCSS variables, iOS/Android constants, etc. One source file → every platform.

Typography System

/* Type scale — consistent rhythm */
:root {{
  --font-family-sans: 'Inter', system-ui, sans-serif;
  --font-family-mono: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
  --font-family-display: 'Cal Sans', sans-serif;
  
  /* Modular scale (1.25 ratio) */
  --text-xs: 0.75rem;    /* 12px */
  --text-sm: 0.875rem;   /* 14px */
  --text-base: 1rem;     /* 16px */
  --text-lg: 1.125rem;   /* 18px */
  --text-xl: 1.25rem;    /* 20px */
  --text-2xl: 1.5rem;    /* 24px */
  --text-3xl: 1.875rem;  /* 30px */
  --text-4xl: 2.25rem;   /* 36px */
  
  /* Line heights */
  --leading-tight: 1.2;   /* headings */
  --leading-normal: 1.6;  /* body text */
  --leading-relaxed: 1.8; /* long-form reading */
  
  /* Font weights */
  --font-normal: 400;
  --font-medium: 500;
  --font-semibold: 600;
  --font-bold: 700;
}}

/* Fluid typography — no breakpoints needed */
h1 {{ font-size: clamp(var(--text-2xl), 5vw, var(--text-4xl)); }}
Use a modular scale (ratio like 1.2 or 1.25) for font sizes. Each size is the previous × ratio. This creates natural visual harmony. Tools: type-scale.com or utopia.fyi for fluid scales.

Spacing & Layout System

/* 4px base spacing scale (Tailwind convention) */
:root {{
  --space-0: 0;
  --space-1: 0.25rem;  /* 4px */
  --space-2: 0.5rem;   /* 8px */
  --space-3: 0.75rem;  /* 12px */
  --space-4: 1rem;     /* 16px */
  --space-6: 1.5rem;   /* 24px */
  --space-8: 2rem;     /* 32px */
  --space-12: 3rem;    /* 48px */
  --space-16: 4rem;    /* 64px */
  --space-24: 6rem;    /* 96px */
}}

/* Usage — consistent spacing everywhere */
.card {{ padding: var(--space-6); gap: var(--space-4); }}
.section {{ margin-block: var(--space-16); }}
.stack > * + * {{ margin-block-start: var(--space-4); }}
A 4px grid means all spacing is a multiple of 4px. This creates visual rhythm and consistency — elements feel aligned and harmonious. When a designer says '16px padding,' you know that's var(--space-4). No magic numbers in your CSS.

Color System Design

Building a Color Palette

1. Brand colors: Primary (actions, links), secondary (accents), tertiary (highlights). Each needs 10 shades (50-950).

2. Neutral grays: 10 shades for text, backgrounds, borders. Slightly tinted with brand color for cohesion.

3. Semantic colors: Success (green), warning (yellow/amber), error/danger (red), info (blue). Each needs background + text + border variants.

4. Surface colors: Background, surface (card bg), elevated surface (modal bg), overlay.

Contrast & Accessibility

/* WCAG AA contrast ratios */
/* Normal text: 4.5:1 minimum */
/* Large text (18px+ or 14px+ bold): 3:1 minimum */
/* Non-text (icons, borders): 3:1 minimum */

/* Test with: Chrome DevTools contrast checker */
/* Or: contrast-ratio.com, polypane.app */

/* Common trap: gray text on white background */
color: #9ca3af; /* ❌ 2.9:1 on white — FAILS AA */
color: #6b7280; /* ✅ 4.6:1 on white — PASSES AA */
Don't just check contrast of your light theme. Dark themes need separate contrast checks — light text on dark backgrounds behaves differently. A color that passes in light mode might fail in dark mode and vice versa.

Motion & Animation Principles

When to Animate

DO animate: State changes (hover, focus, open/close), feedback (success/error), transitions between views, drawing attention to important changes.

DON'T animate: Just for decoration (distracting), critical content (users might miss it), anything that delays user tasks.

Animation Principles

Duration: 100-200ms for micro-interactions (hover, toggle). 200-500ms for medium transitions (modal open, page transition). Nothing over 1s.

Easing: ease-out for entering elements (decelerate into place). ease-in for exiting (accelerate out). ease-in-out for movement. Never linear for UI (feels robotic).

Direction: Content enters from where it came from and exits where it's going. A sidebar slides in from the left and out to the left.

Respect preferences: Always include @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) to disable animations for users who need it.

/* Standardized animation tokens */
:root {{
  --duration-fast: 100ms;
  --duration-normal: 200ms;
  --duration-slow: 400ms;
  --ease-default: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
  --ease-in: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 1, 1);
  --ease-out: cubic-bezier(0, 0, 0.2, 1);
  --ease-bounce: cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1);
}}

@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {{
  *, *::before, *::after {{
    animation-duration: 0.01ms !important;
    transition-duration: 0.01ms !important;
  }}
}}

Common UI patterns, form design, navigation, and real-world design decisions.

Form Design Best Practices

Layout

Single column: Faster to scan and complete than multi-column. Only use multi-column for short, related fields (first name / last name, city / state / zip).

Label placement: Above the field (fastest to scan), not to the left (wastes space, harder to scan). Placeholder text is NOT a label replacement — it disappears on focus.

Field grouping: Use <fieldset> + <legend> for related fields. Visual separation with spacing or subtle borders.

Validation

Validate on blur (when user leaves field), not on every keystroke (annoying). Show errors inline below the field, not in a summary at top. Use positive validation — green checkmark when valid, not just red when invalid.

Error messages: Specific and actionable. Not "Invalid input" but "Email must include @". Associate with aria-describedby for screen readers.

UX Details

Autocomplete: Set autocomplete attribute (email, name, address, tel, cc-number). Browsers auto-fill — massive UX win.

Input types: type="email" shows @ keyboard on mobile. type="tel" shows number pad. inputmode="numeric" for number-only fields.

Submit button: Disable when form is submitting (prevent double-submit). Show loading spinner. Primary action = most prominent button.

Navigation Patterns

Common Patterns

Top navigation bar: Logo left, primary nav center/right, auth/profile far right. Sticky on scroll. Responsive: hamburger menu on mobile.

Side navigation: For dashboards/admin panels. Collapsible (icon-only mode). Persistent across pages. Active state for current page.

Bottom navigation (mobile): 3-5 items max. Icons + labels. Active state. Used by: Instagram, YouTube, Twitter/X. Thumb-friendly zone.

Breadcrumbs: For deep hierarchies (e-commerce categories). Uses aria-label="Breadcrumb" + ol for semantics. Last item is current page (not a link).

Tabs: For switching content within same page. Use role="tablist", role="tab", role="tabpanel". Arrow key navigation between tabs. Only active tab is in tab order.

The 3-click rule is a myth — research shows users don't mind more clicks if each click is clear and confident. Focus on clear information scent (knowing where each click leads) rather than minimizing click count.

Dark Mode Implementation

/* Three-way toggle: light / dark / system */
/* 1. System preference (default) */
@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {{
  :root:not([data-theme]) {{ /* dark tokens */ }}
}}

/* 2. Manual override via data attribute */
[data-theme="light"] {{ /* light tokens */ }}
[data-theme="dark"] {{ /* dark tokens */ }}

/* 3. JavaScript for toggle + persistence */
// Read preference: localStorage.getItem('theme') || 'system'
// Apply: document.documentElement.dataset.theme = theme
// Persist: localStorage.setItem('theme', theme)

Dark Mode Design Tips

Don't just invert: Pure white (#fff) on pure black (#000) is too harsh. Use off-white (#e2e8f0) on dark gray (#0f172a).

Reduce saturation: Vibrant colors look harsh on dark backgrounds. Desaturate or use lighter variants.

Elevation with lightness: In light mode, shadows create depth. In dark mode, lighter surface colors create depth (no visible shadows on dark).

Check contrast separately: Colors that pass WCAG in light mode might fail in dark mode.

Micro-Interactions & Empty States

Micro-Interactions

Small animations that acknowledge user actions: button press (scale down 98%), toggle slide, checkbox bounce, ripple effect, hover lift (translateY -2px + shadow). They add personality and feel 'real.'

Empty States

Never show a blank page. When content is empty, show: 1) What this section is for. 2) Why it's empty. 3) A clear CTA to fix it.

Example: "No projects yet" + illustration + "Create your first project" button. Empty states are onboarding opportunities — guide the user to their first action.

Error States

404 page: Helpful message + search + link to home. Not just "Page not found." 500 page: Apologize + suggest retry + status page link. Offline state: Show cached content with "You're offline" banner + retry button.

UX Fundamentals & Gestalt Principles

Understanding human psychology to create intuitive, frictionless interfaces.

The Gestalt Principles of Perception

Gestalt psychology explains how the human brain automatically organizes visual elements into groups or unified wholes.

  • Proximity: Elements placed close to each other are perceived as related. This is the most powerful grouping principle. Use negative space (whitespace) to group logical sections.
  • Similarity: Elements that look alike (color, shape, size) are perceived as having the same function. If all your primary buttons are blue, users will click a blue banner expecting an action.
  • Continuity: The eye is compelled to move through one object and continue to another. Used heavily in carousels and horizontal scrolling lists to guide the user's vision off-screen to indicate more content.
  • Closure: The brain prefers complete shapes. If you show an incomplete icon or a clipped image, the brain fills in the missing visual gaps automatically.
Fitts's Law & Hick's Law
  • Fitts's Law: The time required to rapidly move to a target area is a function of the distance to the target and the size of the target. Translation: Make primary action buttons large and place them close to where the user's cursor/thumb already is. Edges and corners of screens are "infinitely" deep targets for a mouse.
  • Hick's Law: The time it takes to make a decision increases with the number and complexity of choices. Translation: Don't put 15 links in a dropdown. Group choices into a hierarchy, or hide advanced options behind a "More" button to reduce cognitive load.

Micro-Interactions & Component Design

The tiny details that separate merely functional apps from premium, delightful experiences.

Designing State

A button isn't just a rectangle; it is a state machine. Every interactive component must account for at least 5 states:

  • Default: The baseline appearance.
  • Hover: Visual feedback that the element is interactive (cursor changes, slight shadow, color shift). Note: Hover does not exist on mobile.
  • Focus: Critical for keyboard accessibility. Must have a high-contrast outline indicating this is the keyboard target.
  • Active / Pressed: Physical feedback that the click is registering (a physical push-down effect or ripple).
  • Disabled: Indicates the action is currently unachievable, usually via low opacity and a `not-allowed` cursor. Pro UX tip: Avoid disabled buttons if possible; instead, leave them enabled and show a helpful error explaining *why* it failed when clicked.
Color Theory & Contrast
  • The 60-30-10 Rule: A classic design rule. 60% of the UI should be the dominant color (usually white/dark gray background), 30% a secondary color (cards, sidebars), and 10% an accent color (primary buttons, links).
  • WCAG Contrast Ratio: Text must have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 against its background for normal text (WCAG AA). Never use medium-gray text on a light-gray background, it is physically inaccessible to millions.
  • Meaningful Color: Never use color alone to convey meaning. Colorblind users cannot differentiate a red error state from a green success state easily—always include a supporting icon (✅ or ❌).

UX/UI Architecture & Design Systems

Building resilient, developer-friendly, and accessible design systems for enterprise scale.

Design Token Architecture

Decouple styling from components for multi-platform consistency.

  • Tier 1: Global/Core Tokens (e.g., --blue-500: #3b82f6).
  • Tier 2: Alias/Semantic Tokens (e.g., --action-primary: var(--blue-500)).
  • Tier 3: Component-Specific Tokens (e.g., --btn-bg: var(--action-primary)).

Advanced Accessibility (a11y)

Going beyond the basics for a truly inclusive experience.

  • Cognitive Accessibility: Reducing cognitive load with consistent patterns, minimal distractions, and clear multi-modal feedback.
  • Focus Management: Capturing focus in modals, correctly using aria-live for dynamic updates, and ensuring logical tab order for keyboard users.
  • User Preference Queries: Respecting prefers-reduced-motion, prefers-contrast, and prefers-color-scheme.

Enterprise Design Systems (Atomic Design)

Managing components as a hierarchy of complexity.

Atoms & Molecules

Buttons, inputs, icons (Atoms) combined into Search bars, Cards, or Modals (Molecules). These are the primitives of your system.

Organisms & Templates

Complex reusable sections (Headers, Navigation menus, Data tables) that form the layouts (Templates) where real content is injected.

// Example of Headless UI pattern for components
const Tabs = ({ children }) => {{
  const [active, setActive] = useState(0);
  // Provide state via context, but NO styles.
  // Consumer brings their own styling (Tailwind, Modules, etc).
  return <TabsContext.Provider value={{...}}>{{children}}</TabsContext.Provider>;
}}

Design for Performance (UX-P)

How UI design choices directly impact modern performance metrics like INP (Interaction to Next Paint).

  • Skeleton Screens: Perceived performance vs real performance. Using CSS transforms rather than width/height for layout-stable loading states.
  • Optimistic UI: Instantly updating the UI before server confirmation (e.g., \"liking\" a post). Requires complex error-rollback logic.
  • Variable Fonts: Reducing font-weight bloat. Loading one font file for 100-900 weights instead of 5 separate files. Massive LCP win.
BASIC What is the difference between UX and UI? +

UX (User Experience) is the analytical logic, psychology, and feel of the experience (wireframes, research, user journeys). UI (User Interface) is the visual skin, typography, colors, and layout (the aesthetic presentation).

BASIC What is a Call to Action (CTA)? +

A prominent button or link designed to prompt an immediate response or encourage an immediate sale (e.g., "Sign Up Now" or "Buy It").

BASIC What is the 60-30-10 color rule? +

A classic design principle for creating a balanced color palette. 60% is your dominant hue (usually background), 30% is your secondary color, and 10% is your accent color (CTAs and highlights).

BASIC What are wireframes vs mockups vs prototypes? +

Wireframes are low-fidelity structural blueprints (gray boxes). Mockups are high-fidelity static visual designs (colors and fonts). Prototypes are interactive models simulating the final flow.

BASIC Why is Typography important in UI? +

It establishes visual hierarchy, guides eye tracking, heavily impacts readability, and sets the entire emotional tone and brand identity of an application.

BASIC What is Affordance in design? +

A physical characteristic of an object or interface that visually hints at how it should be used. For example, a 3D shadow on a button affords clicking. A slider affords dragging.

BASIC What is Mobile-First Design? +

Designing the UI and UX for the smallest screen (mobile) first, focusing purely on core features, and progressively enhancing the layout for larger tablet and desktop screens.

BASIC What does accessibility mean in UI design? +

Ensuring your design is usable by people with disabilities. This means maintaining high color contrast, ensuring keyboard navigability, and providing screen-reader labels.

INTERMEDIATE Explain Fitts's Law. +

The time required to move to a target is a function of the distance to the target and the size of the target. Therefore, primary actions should be large and close to the user's natural starting position.

INTERMEDIATE Explain Hick's Law. +

The time it takes to make a decision increases with the number and complexity of choices. In UX, this means you should minimize the number of options (e.g. navigation links) presented at once to prevent paralysis.

INTERMEDIATE What is the Gestalt Principle of Proximity? +

Items physically close to each other are perceived as being grouped or related to one another. It is the strongest way to establish relationships without drawing literal bounding boxes.

ADVANCED What is the Aesthetic-Usability Effect? +

A psychological phenomenon where users perceive more aesthetically pleasing designs to be more intuitive and easier to use than less pleasing designs, even if the actual usability is identical.

ADVANCED Describe Skeleton Screens vs Spinners. +

Skeleton screens display a blank version of a page into which information is gradually loaded, reducing the user's *perceived* wait time. Spinners just show a loading wheel and increase frustration for long loads.

ADVANCED What are Dark Patterns? +

UI and UX design choices specifically engineered to trick users into doing things they did not intend to do, like sneaking items into a cart or making the "Cancel Subscription" button invisible.

EXPERT What is Cognitive Load in UX? +

The total amount of mental effort being used in the working memory. Extraneous cognitive load generated by cluttered UI, unclear labels, and disruptive pop-ups severely degrades user success rates.

ADVANCED Explain Heuristic Evaluation. +

A usability engineering method where evaluators examine the UI and judge its compliance with recognized usability principles (the "heuristics"), such as Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics.

EXPERT How do you design for the "Fat Finger" problem? +

Touch targets on mobile devices must be a minimum of 44x44 CSS pixels (according to Apple/WCAG) to ensure users can accurately tap them without hitting adjacent interactive elements.

EXPERT What is a Design System vs a UI Library? +

A UI Library is purely a collection of coded components. A Design System is a complete set of standards, documentation, design tokens, voice & tone guidelines, and the UI library, intended to manage design at scale.

EXPERT What is the "Paradox of Choice" in UX? +

An observation that providing too many options can lead to anxiety and decision paralysis, eventually leading users to abandon the task entirely. Mitigation: Categorization, progressive disclosure, and smart defaults.

EXPERT Explain "Mental Models" and "Conceptual Models". +

A mental model is what the user *thinks* they know about how a system works. A conceptual model is the actual design as implemented. UX friction occurs when these two models do not align.

EXPERT What is "Emotional Design" (Don Norman)? +

Designing for three levels of processing: Visceral (automatic response to aesthetics), Behavioral (satisfaction with usability), and Reflective (conscious thought about brand and meaning).

EXPERT What are "Micro-interactions" and why do they matter? +

Tiny functional movements (like a button ripple or a success checkmark animation) that provide instant feedback. They humanize the interface and prevent user uncertainty.

EXPERT Explain the "Peak-End Rule" in UX Research. +

Users judge an experience largely based on how they felt at its peak (most intense point) and at its end, rather than the average of every moment of the experience.

EXPERT What is "Service Design" vs "Product Design"? +

Product design focuses on a specific software artifact. Service design maps the entire end-to-end journey across all touchpoints (digital, physical, human) to improve the overall service quality.

EXPERT Compare Contextual Inquiry vs User Interviews. +

User interviews are semi-structured conversations. Contextual Inquiry involves observing the user in their actual environment doing their actual work, uncovering behaviors that users might not mention in an interview.

EXPERT What is a "Card Sort" test? +

A research method where users organize information into groups that make sense to them. Generative: users create their own categories. Closed: users sort into predefined categories. Used to design Information Architecture.

EXPERT Explain "Affordance" vs "Signifiers". +

Affordance is the *possibility* of an action (a chair affords sitting). A signifier is the visual signal that tells the user the action is possible (a button's shadow is a signifier of its affordance to be clicked).

EXPERT What is "Design Debt"? +

The accrued cost of poor design decisions (shortcuts, inconsistencies) that result in a more difficult user experience and slower development speed over time.

EXPERT What is "System Usability Scale" (SUS)? +

A quick, reliable 10-item questionnaire used to measure the perceived usability of a system. It provides a score from 0-100 that can be benchmarked against industry averages.

EXPERT Explain "Atomic Design" methodology. +

A system for building interfaces by Brad Frost: Atoms (labels, inputs) -> Molecules (search form) -> Organisms (header) -> Templates (page structure) -> Pages (real content).

EXPERT What is "Eye Tracking" and when should it be used? +

A research tool that measures where users look on a screen. Used to identify "blind spots", verify visual hierarchy, and see if critical CTAs are actually being noticed.

EXPERT What are "User Journey Maps" vs "User Flows"? +

A User Flow is a linear diagram of steps a user takes through the UI. A Journey Map is a high-level visualization of the user's emotions, motivations, and pain points across the entire experience.

EXPERT What is the "Doherty Threshold"? +

The observation that productivity skyrockets when a computer and its users interact at a pace (< 400ms) that ensures neither has to wait on the other. It defines the UX requirement for "instant" feedback.

28 interview questions from very basic to very advanced. Click to reveal answers.

Showing 28 questions
BWhat is UX vs UI?+

UX (User Experience): How the product feels — the entire journey. Includes research, information architecture, user flows, wireframes, usability testing. UI (User Interface): How it looks — visual design. Colors, typography, icons, spacing, animations. UX is the blueprint; UI is the paint and furnishing. Both are essential; UX without good UI feels clunky, UI without good UX is pretty but unusable.

BWhat are design tokens?+

Named design decisions stored as data: colors, spacing, typography, shadows, border radius, animation durations. They're the atomic values of a design system. Stored as CSS custom properties, JSON, or JS constants. Benefits: single source of truth, enable theming, sync between Figma and code, platform-agnostic (same tokens → CSS, iOS, Android).

BWhat is a design system?+

A collection of reusable components, design tokens, guidelines, and patterns that ensure consistency across products. Includes: 1) Design tokens (colors, spacing). 2) Component library (buttons, inputs, cards). 3) Documentation (usage guidelines, do's and don'ts). 4) Patterns (forms, navigation, data display). Examples: Material Design (Google), Carbon (IBM), Radix + shadcn/ui.

BWhat is visual hierarchy?+

Guiding the user's eye to the most important content first. Achieved through: Size (larger = more important), Color (high contrast = attention), Weight (bold text stands out), Spacing (whitespace isolates important elements), Position (top-left is read first in LTR), Depth (shadows/elevation). Primary CTA should be the most visually prominent element.

BWhat is whitespace (negative space)?+

Empty space between and around elements. NOT wasted space — it's a design tool. Benefits: improves readability, creates visual grouping (related items closer together), reduces cognitive load, feels premium/clean. Apple's design is famous for generous whitespace. Rule: when in doubt, add more space.

BWhat is the difference between wireframe, mockup, and prototype?+

Wireframe: Low-fidelity layout showing structure/content placement. No colors, real content, or interactivity. Focuses on information architecture. Mockup: High-fidelity static design with real colors, typography, images. Shows final visual appearance. Prototype: Interactive simulation of the product. Clickable flows, animations, real interactions. Tools: wireframe (Balsamiq), mockup/prototype (Figma).

BWhat is color contrast and why does it matter?+

The difference in luminance between foreground and background. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text. Low contrast = hard to read for everyone, impossible for visually impaired users. Test with Chrome DevTools (inspect element → color picker shows ratio). About 8% of men and 0.5% of women have color vision deficiency — never rely on color alone.

BWhat is the F-pattern?+

How users scan web pages: two horizontal sweeps at the top, then a vertical scan down the left side — forming an 'F'. Important content should be in: the first line (headline), the second line (subheadline/key info), and the left side of the page. This is why left-aligned text and top-loaded content work best.

BWhat is responsive design?+

Designing one product that works on all screen sizes. Principles: fluid layouts (%, fr, flex), media queries for breakpoints, responsive images (srcset), mobile-first approach (design for small screens first, enhance for larger). Modern: container queries (components adapt to their container), clamp() for fluid values, auto-fit/auto-fill grid columns.

BWhat is a CTA (Call to Action)?+

The element that prompts the user to take the desired action — sign up, buy, subscribe, start trial. Should be: visually prominent (contrasting color, large), clearly worded (action verb: 'Get Started' not 'Submit'), placed where users naturally look (above fold, end of content). One primary CTA per view — too many choices = no choice (Hick's Law).

IExplain Fitts's Law and its UX implications.+

The time to reach a target is a function of the distance to the target and the size of the target. Larger targets + closer placement = faster interaction. Implications: make primary buttons bigger, place frequent actions near cursor/thumb position, increase clickable area (padding on links/buttons), corners/edges of screen are 'infinite' targets (easy to reach). Mobile: bottom nav is faster than top nav (thumb zone).

IWhat is Hick's Law?+

Decision time increases with the number of choices. More options = longer to decide = higher chance of abandoning. Implications: limit nav items (5-7 max), progressive disclosure (show basic options first, advanced behind 'more'), categorize long lists, recommend a default option, reduce form fields to essentials. Amazon's 1-Click Buy is Hick's Law in action — zero decisions.

IWhat is Gestalt theory in design?+

How humans perceive visual groups: Proximity: Close items seem related. Similarity: Similar items seem grouped. Continuity: Eyes follow lines/curves. Closure: Brain completes incomplete shapes. Figure/Ground: Distinguishing foreground from background. Used in: card layouts (proximity), icon sets (similarity), progress indicators (continuity), logos (closure).

IHow do you implement dark mode properly?+

1) Token-based: All colors via CSS variables, swap token values per theme. 2) Three-way toggle: Light / Dark / System (respects prefers-color-scheme). 3) Persist choice: localStorage + cookie (for SSR). 4) Design adjustments: Lower saturation, use off-white not pure white, lighter surfaces = elevated, reduce shadows. 5) Separate contrast checks: Colors that pass in light may fail in dark. 6) Flash prevention: Set theme in script before body renders (inline blocking script).

IWhat is a component library vs design system?+

Component library: A collection of reusable UI components (buttons, inputs, modals). Code only. Design system: A component library PLUS: design tokens, usage guidelines, design principles, accessibility standards, interaction patterns, documentation, Figma components, and a governance process. A design system is a product that serves your product teams.

IWhat is the token hierarchy (primitive → semantic → component)?+

Primitive/Global: Raw values with no meaning: --blue-500: #3b82f6. Semantic/Alias: Purpose-driven names: --color-primary: var(--blue-600). Component: Usage-specific: --button-bg: var(--color-primary). This layering lets you: change the blue shade globally (primitive), change what 'primary' means (semantic), or customize one component (component) — without touching other layers.

IHow do you handle icon systems?+

SVG sprites: Define icons in a single SVG with <symbol> elements, reference with <use href='#icon-name'>. React icon components: Each icon is a component with props for size, color, title (accessibility). Icon fonts: Legacy approach (Font Awesome) — harder to style, poor accessibility. Modern: use SVG inline or sprite. Sizing: Token-based (16px, 20px, 24px). Color: currentColor inherits text color.

IWhat is progressive disclosure?+

Show only what's necessary at each step, revealing more as needed. Examples: 'Advanced settings' toggle, accordion FAQ, tooltip help text, multi-step wizard instead of one massive form, 'Show more' on long lists. Benefits: reduces cognitive load, focuses attention, doesn't overwhelm beginners while still serving power users.

IHow do you design accessible forms?+

Labels: Every input needs a visible <label> linked with for=. Errors: Inline below field, linked with aria-describedby, announced with aria-live. Required fields: required attribute + visual indicator (asterisk in label, not just color). Field groups: <fieldset> + <legend>. Keyboard: Tab order matches visual order, Enter submits. Autocomplete: Set autocomplete attributes for browser autofill.

IWhat are skeleton screens and when to use them?+

Grey placeholder shapes that mimic the layout of content being loaded. Better than spinners because they: set expectations for content layout, reduce perceived load time (users feel the page loaded faster), prevent layout shift (CLS). Use for: card lists, dashboards, profile pages — anything with a predictable layout. Don't use for: content with unpredictable layout, or very fast loads (<200ms).

IWhat is the difference between Material Design and Human Interface Guidelines?+

Material Design (Google): Paper metaphor, elevation with shadows, bold colors, ripple animations, 8dp grid, density levels. Platform: Android + web. HIG (Apple): Clarity (readable text, clear icons), deference (content first, chrome minimal), depth (layers, blur, translucency). Platform: iOS + macOS. Both are comprehensive but opinionated differently. Web apps often create their own design language drawing from both.

AHow do you build a theming system that supports multiple brands?+

Architecture: Three-layer tokens: global primitives → brand-specific semantic tokens → component tokens. Each brand overrides the semantic layer: [data-brand='acme'] maps --color-primary to their blue, while [data-brand='globex'] maps it to their purple. Components only reference semantic tokens. Build: Style Dictionary transforms one JSON source per brand into CSS. Runtime: Load brand CSS dynamically via media/data attribute. Testing: Storybook with brand selector addon for visual comparison.

AHow would you design and maintain a design system for a large organization?+

Team: Dedicated design system team (designers + engineers). Process: RFC for new components, design review + code review + a11y audit before merge. Versioning: Semver, changelog, deprecation warnings (one major version before removal). Documentation: Storybook with usage guidelines, do's/don'ts, API reference. Adoption: Codemods for breaking changes, migration guides, office hours for teams. Metrics: Track adoption rate, bug reports, satisfaction surveys. Governance: Clear contribution guidelines, regular audits, design/code token sync.

AExplain the concept of design tokens pipeline (from Figma to code).+

1) Figma: Designers define tokens using Figma Variables (colors, spacing, typography). 2) Export: Tokens plugin exports to JSON/YAML format. 3) Transform: Style Dictionary or Tokens Studio processes JSON into platform outputs: CSS variables, SCSS, JS/TS constants, iOS Swift, Android Kotlin. 4) Publish: Token package published to npm (or CDN). 5) Consume: Apps import tokens. 6) Sync: CI/CD triggers on token changes → auto-PR to consuming repos. Key: the JSON is the single source of truth, everything downstream is generated.

AHow do you measure and improve usability?+

Quantitative: Task success rate, time on task, error rate, SUS (System Usability Scale) score, NPS. Measured via analytics and A/B tests. Qualitative: Usability testing (5 users find 85% of issues), user interviews, heuristic evaluation (Nielsen's 10), cognitive walkthrough. Continuous: Session replay (Hotjar, FullStory), heatmaps, click maps, rage click detection. Process: Test early (wireframe testing), test often (every sprint), test with real users (not just team members). Iterate based on data, not opinions.

AHow do you handle design system versioning and breaking changes?+

Semver: Major (breaking), minor (new features), patch (fixes). Deprecation policy: Mark deprecated in current major, remove in next major (minimum 3 months). Visual deprecation warnings in Storybook and console. Codemods: Auto-transform old API to new: npx @myds/codemod button-v2. Migration guide: Step-by-step with before/after code. Canary releases: Let teams test before stable. Version dashboard: Track which teams are on which version.

AWhat is the Radix/shadcn approach and why has it become popular?+

Radix: Unstyled, accessible primitive components (Dialog, Dropdown, Tabs, etc.) with complete keyboard/ARIA support. You add your own styles. shadcn/ui: Copy-paste components built on Radix + Tailwind. Not a dependency — code lives in your project. Why popular: Full control over styling (no fighting a library's CSS), components are yours to modify, accessible by default, works with any styling approach. This 'headless UI + local components' pattern has replaced installed component libraries for many teams.

AHow do you ensure design-engineering consistency?+

Shared tokens: Designers and engineers use the SAME token values (Figma Variables synced to CSS variables). Component parity: Every Figma component has a code counterpart with matching props/variants. Design reviews: Engineers review designs for feasibility; designers review implementations for accuracy. Storybook: Living documentation that both teams reference. Visual regression: Chromatic catches unintended visual changes. Handoff: Figma Dev Mode provides exact specs, not PDF mocks.
